pathophysiology_summary: Giant cell arteritis is a granulomatous large- and medium-vessel vasculitis affecting the branches of the external carotid artery (notably the superficial temporal artery) and the ophthalmic/posterior ciliary arteries. Inflammatory infiltration of the vessel wall causes luminal narrowing and ischaemia: temporal headache and scalp tenderness (superficial temporal artery), jaw claudication (ischaemia of the masseter via the maxillary artery), and — most feared — anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy or central retinal artery occlusion causing sudden, usually irreversible, blindness. Systemic inflammation drives fever, fatigue and weight loss, with markedly elevated ESR/CRP, and an overlap with polymyalgia rheumatica (proximal girdle stiffness). It is a rheumatological and ophthalmological emergency: high-dose glucocorticoids must be started on the SAME DAY that GCA is suspected — before biopsy — because established visual loss rarely recovers. Temporal artery biopsy (or vascular ultrasound) confirms the diagnosis but must not delay treatment.

differential.evidence_basis: New-onset unilateral temporal headache with scalp tenderness in a patient over 50 — GCA must be actively excluded.
Common cause of headache, but does not explain jaw claudication, scalp tenderness or systemic features.
New severe unilateral throbbing headache — but new-onset migraine at 72 is unusual and does not fit jaw claudication.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening complaint alone, GCA should already lead the differential given age plus a new temporal headache — anchoring on 'primary headache' is the trap.

differential.evidence_basis: Jaw claudication + scalp tenderness + new temporal headache + systemic symptoms in an older adult — a highly specific cluster.
Trigeminal neuralgia is brief, sharp, electric-shock pain triggered by touch — not a continuous throbbing ache with systemic inflammation.
TMJ dysfunction causes mechanical joint pain/clicking without systemic inflammatory signs or scalp tenderness; jaw pain in GCA is ischaemic fatigue relieved by rest.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Jaw claudication (ischaemic fatigue relieved by rest) is the single most specific historical feature — its presence should move GCA to a near-certain working diagnosis pending bloods/biopsy.

differential.diagnosis: Giant cell arteritis without current visual involvement
differential.evidence_basis: Classic cranial GCA cluster; visual symptoms currently absent (DI-006) — a narrow window in which same-day steroids can prevent blindness.

differential.diagnosis: Anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (GCA-related, if vision affected)
differential.evidence_basis: The dreaded complication — any new visual symptom means ischaemic optic neuropathy is occurring and mandates emergency treatment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The absence of visual symptoms is reassuring for CURRENT status but does NOT lower urgency — it defines the same-day treatment window. Any new visual symptom is an emergency.

differential.diagnosis: Giant cell arteritis (temporal arteritis), presumptive — pending ESR/CRP and temporal artery biopsy
differential.evidence_basis: Pathognomonic cranial GCA constellation in a patient over 50; treatment (high-dose glucocorticoids) must start same-day, before confirmatory testing.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm GCA (no palpation, no ESR/CRP, no biopsy) — the correct output is a strong suspicion driving urgent same-day in-person assessment and treatment, not a remote confirmation.

red_flags.description: New-onset temporal headache in a patient over 50, unrelieved by simple analgesia
red_flags.clinical_significance: A new temporal headache after 50 is the sentinel symptom of GCA and mandates active exclusion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to consider GCA leads to delayed steroids and preventable, usually irreversible, blindness.

red_flags.description: Scalp tenderness (burning scalp on brushing hair / lying on pillow)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates inflammation of the superficial temporal / scalp arteries — supports cranial GCA.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing this feature weakens the pattern recognition that should trigger same-day treatment.

red_flags.description: Jaw claudication — ischaemic jaw-muscle pain/fatigue on chewing, relieved by rest
red_flags.clinical_significance: The most specific historical feature of GCA and a marker of higher ischaemic (including ocular) risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Overlooking jaw claudication is the classic way GCA is mistaken for a benign headache or TMJ problem.

red_flags.description: Visual symptoms — currently ABSENT and screened negative (no blurring, diplopia, transient/permanent visual loss, or curtain)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absence of visual loss defines the same-day window to prevent it; any NEW visual symptom is an ophthalmic emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to screen and safety-net for visual symptoms risks the patient not recognising sight-threatening progression.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling a new temporal headache in an older adult as tension headache or migraine and missing the GCA pattern.
Attributing jaw pain on chewing to a mechanical joint or dental cause rather than recognising ischaemic claudication.
Delaying high-dose glucocorticoids until ESR/CRP or temporal artery biopsy results are available — treatment must start the same day GCA is suspected.
Being reassured that vision is currently intact and under-triaging — normal vision defines the treatment window, it does not reduce urgency.
Attempting to confirm or exclude GCA remotely; palpation of the temporal artery, visual assessment and ESR/CRP are not obtainable via chat.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the temporal artery (hardness, nodularity, tenderness, diminished/absent pulse)
Formal visual acuity, visual fields to confrontation, and relative afferent pupillary defect
Fundoscopy for anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy
ESR and CRP (expected markedly elevated) and full blood count
Temporal artery biopsy or temporal/axillary artery ultrasound
Objective vital signs (BP, HR, temperature, RR, SpO2) without a validated home device
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise suspected giant cell arteritis and arrange URGENT SAME-DAY in-person assessment (GP/urgent care or ED) to start high-dose glucocorticoids immediately and obtain urgent ESR/CRP/FBC, with urgent rheumatology and ophthalmology referral for temporal artery biopsy and formal eye assessment. Escalate to emergency (000 / emergency eye service) immediately for ANY new visual symptom.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the pattern (the constellation is classic); the real risk is UNDER-action — treating it as a benign headache, or delaying same-day steroids pending investigations, either of which can cost the patient's sight.

guidelines_referenced.reference: BSR Guideline for the diagnosis and treatment of giant cell arteritis (2020)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspected GCA is a medical emergency; start high-dose glucocorticoids immediately (do not wait for biopsy) and refer urgently for specialist assessment and temporal artery biopsy/ultrasound within days.

guidelines_referenced.reference: EULAR recommendations for the management of large vessel vasculitis (2018)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: In suspected large vessel vasculitis, initiate high-dose glucocorticoid therapy promptly; confirm with imaging or biopsy without delaying treatment.
